result:
The other night, near midnight, I stepped out onto the fire-escape in order to decide whether I still lived in the same city I thought I lived in. New York specializes in self-reinvention, but that is ordinarily a solid, daylight project—steel rising, facades reborn, zoning hearings where citizens shout. What struck me in the dark, though, was not the audible jackhammer but its opposite: the silence that replaces a giant piece of terra-cotta balcony that used to hold rain in the shape of stone acanthus leaves, now held together instead by mesh safety netting the workers forgot to remove. Under that industrial cocoon, nothing stirred. Somewhere between the nineteenth-century tenements and the twenty-first-century glass box directly across the street, an unmarked interval of time had opened up, and I found myself living inside it.

That pocket of historical silence is, in miniature, the condition of the present. No moment of the past is ever fully gone, and the future comes bristling with the invoices of the past: utility bills, algorithmic debts, thyroid counts from atmospheric testing, DNA loops it was easier to suture than to delete. Every place you stand is sedimentary paper layered so evenly you one atop another that you feel depth only at the edges, like pages cut in error and leaving a glimpse of something earlier. You flip the page and older ink bleeds through: a concentration-camp blueprint, a children’s menu from a Concord resort in 1927, shipping labels for king-crab legs, protest photos from right now shared with you by someone in another time zone. The stack is unreadable en masse; by lexis it is encyclopedic.

I have been thinking about layering because I am making bread again after a fifteen-year hiatus and the rhythm of folding dough reintroduces me to the documentary nature of repetition; every time you press the heel of your hand into the dough and pull the far edge toward you, you leave a little claw-print of autobiography. This dough will rise overnight, which means that somewhere in its genome of gluten and yeast the hours I spend asleep will be translated into taste. If I let the dough sit in the refrigerator for a week, it gains a faint vinegar sting; if I stretch it with olive oil and bake it hard, it becomes a commercial-grade cracker. The sour note becomes a chronicle of private patience. I am not in control of its bacterial parliament; my only office is temperature manager and cloudy-gazer.

Bread is only flour and water extended in time the way sound is only air extended in time, but both produce a metabolic echo inside us, pleasure that begins long before the phrase reaches the brain. The kind of bread I am making is entirely unglamorous: 75 percent hydration, 80 percent white bread flour, 20 percent red whole wheat, teaspoon of salt, dollop of levain older than some friendships. When the slab first emerges it looks like a down jacket deflating—proof that expansiveness can also be temporary. I carve my initials inside with the lame, though an hour later the seam has scarred over and will not shout anymore. The crust, if you know how to listen, sings—a high glissando as the starch retrogrades and cracks open lattice lines—so a loaf of bread is also a piece of porous choir. The apartment smells gamy enough to send an imaginary dog howling.

But memory. The smell now drags a thread back thirty-five years to a summer morning in a bakery on the Via degli Orefici in Bologna, in which I—nine years old, half Ferrara dialect, half American curiosity—watched a man pound dough as big as a suitcase against floured marble, the slap echoing off medieval vaulting. That sound combined with the cathedral intensity of light coming through the doorway, sliced gold by hanging mozzarella, first gave me the idea that labor could be a form of hieroglyphics. The man’s forearm bore tattoos rendering anchors and naked women in blue; my Ohio grandparents thought tattoos were things of secrets only soldiers kept; I did not yet know how porous private rituals become public stories. The man’s hands were bread; his hands were stories; he delivered a ciabatta to the counter steaming so turbulently that the crust blistered under a film of condensation you could map like relief topography. I never ate such a ciabatta again, but the years have brought successive approximants, and each one is a rewriting and also an apology to that original.

This is how heritage works, not blood but bakery. I belong to three places at once: the wheat bisons of North American prairies milled by unpronounceable conglomerates, the Adriatic salt left by retreating Panonian clay, and the miraculous fungi mingling immigrant sourdoughs in a jar on a Brooklyn windowsill. When I opened that jar this morning a puff of sulfur suggested that some complicated negotiation occurs nightly between Ukrainian flours and Chinatown’s octave of yeasts. The account is forever open-ended. Heritage does not ask permission; it simply balances its guttural debts across time zones. In that respect the levain behaves like the internet’s algorithmic unconscious, digesting fragments of human speech and retching them back as sponsored articles, except that the levain never forgets to stop before it blackens into ink.

Sometimes I speak to the jar. (One must animate what otherwise the ghost of pasteurization creeps.) Hello, I say, how did the October go? Has anyone written you into folklore yet? Did the apartment’s humidity attend the meeting? A levain does not represent a knowable population count but rather a momentum, a tendency. When I fail to feed it after eight days it subsides and acquires a medieval sobriety. A weaker person would mourn the injured child of civilization. I revive it anyway. The resurrection smells like bananas and alcohol and rennet: biological metaphysics. It goes back to sleep behind an elastic band and a crossed sign of prefectural origin.

If a boy can inherit a voice composed of wheat and foam and a Borgesian city at night, then surely he can inherit a book of landlessness too, a passport of questions. When I left the Midwest for good—instead of the weekend, instead of the summer—my grandfather cuff-linked my fifteen-year-old shoulder and said, The thing about getting far away is you get close to home second-hand. He meant maps. He meant that the world is wrapped around the globe like the glutinous shell of a dumpling, and once you pierce it every region leaks a broth that tastes familiar no matter where you draw the knife. His own generation had gone to war to return with photostatic memories clicked into stereoscopic viewers: St. Paul’s dome unscarred, Brandenburg Gate with no wall behind it, cherry trees in the Maritime provinces raining on submarines. In those reels I first learned the second person plural— we, the viewers against sorrow—but the syntactic fuse shivered when they clicked to the next slide: rows of nameless Korean women filing past a rail depot. The collective pronoun corkscrewed into private vertigo. A bird the color of winter flew across the ceiling. That vertigo has kept returning like yeast notes that cannot be proven accountable for anything yet reassert themselves in my dreams of train stations.

The paradox of distance is distance itself remains a place subject to tourism. Take Greenland. I have never been. On map apps it appears as an exaggerated inhale, an ice lung crushed between continents. Satellite imagery renders it baby-blue even in winter, a color that occurs nowhere inside our retinas except through prisms. In corporate pitches Greenland is simultaneously mineral heap and last Eden; in video streams young people set up camping stoves on tidewater fronts that no longer freeze. I discover that among NASA climate boards “the greening of Greenland” is a private joke wrapped in an epitaph. Yet my thumb keeps scrolling until the glacier face becomes wallpaper for a phone that is heating up to exactly the temperature recommended for proofing dough. Somewhere the screen overlays a notification: “Your yeast is ready.” My brain stitches two unrelated latitudes into one irrational whole: can I bake bread whose crumb mirrors a calving glacier? Could the steam registers be tuned to tidal chronology, so that every pocket of fermentation records the audible pop of methane bubbles bursting in thawing permafrost? The recipe would need salt mined from the tears of sixteenth-century Basque cod fishermen, who once watched their wooden ovens warp and leak in the storm off Labrador. Yet here I am again, tethered by a copper wire running inside a kitchen wall older than the idea of pixels.

I sometimes suspect that the bare linguistic phrase “I don’t know where I am going” has more mileage than any life plan. Not-knowing is the black truffle folded into the sauce: valuable precisely because it is underground, risky if overused, overrated if subjected to promiscuous fetish. When you taste it you taste the baobab of mystery in one molecule, and then it finishes into coarser prosaic bedding—that is what nostalgia does to time. In high school we had a physics teacher who spent one entire period drawing on the whiteboard a triangle the way textbooks draw an iceberg: 90 percent below the water of consciousness. “That,” he said, “is the future.” One bold student asked what the ten percent above represented. The teacher moved the marker across the surface to leave a circular hole. “Today,” he answered. The classroom smelled of green marker and jealousy, as though knowledge belonged only to vertices and everything else was irritating static. But I smuggled away a question that has not stopped growing: if the triangle were placed on graph paper, what unit marks the ordinate of missing knowledge? Only later did I realize the teacher had borrowed the metaphor from whatever English department was failing him: after all, for the Titanic the visible future lasted precisely forty seconds after first impact.

That same winter my friend Sarah typed a poem onto dot-matrix printer paper torn into strips and taped to the lockers. The strips read as single words—beach, blood, porcelain, polio—at vertical intervals, so that you encountered them one jog at a time on the way to chemistry lab. We understood her target was the escalator muscle we use to ascend from locker row toward adulthood, every step aerating another graveyard vowel. When taped back together, the strips formed a longer poem about a blind girl waking on shore to a tide of syringes washed upstream from a hospital barge. It was schlock sensationalism. The principal threatened expulsion. But held up to the fluorescent ceiling the long sheet became translucent and the cryptic spaces between lines let through an older light: someone’s grandmother’s handwriting in carbon copies of ration recipes from Leningrad 1942. The laminate reflected the future backwards into the past, and honesty slipped out only as residue. The poem’s ingredient list: scarcity plus embarrassment equals voice. We never discovered who rescued the shreds; they disappeared over weekend. Still the locker room smelled of soldered memory, a faintly erroneous brine not unlike the lava-salt I now use to cover focaccia fingerprints before baking.

Years after, in London, I found the echo in the Churchill War Rooms, where the acoustics carry the twenty-four-hour metronome of a twentieth-century switchboard operator who once plugged cables for RAF fighter commands. An elderly guard pointed out the grease stains behind a map of the Barents Sea that lasted across the war. The stains were caused by the elbow of the woman who had to sit so close she wore the paper away without noticing. That woman left no diary. Only the negative impression. What is biography that is not text; biography that is a scratch in absence. I bought a postcard on the way out: same map, minus her elbow, commercially wholesome. The dictum “Never Forget” has always meant that forgetting is the curated item. I carried a little plastic bag of edible souvenirs: a piece of stout-soaked Welsh cheddar whose rind retained burlap threads, plus a booklet of postage stamps commemorating extinct beetle species. They rattled in my pocket next to an Oyster card that believed it contained £1.60. Late on the Piccadilly line a girl unfolded a paper sculpture from behind her ear—cranes made of receipts—and handed them to strangers, each bird stamped with the words Forgive me I didn’t mean to overhear. Nobody spoke the typo. By the time she reached the end of the carriage the birds had formed a small migrating flock under the strip lighting. I understood then that souvenirs are messages you send forward to yourself from the exact point you forgot what you were supposed to understand.

Bread again because dough does not pretend to contain a moral lesson. I stretch it on parchment into a rectangle. The digital scale shows grams like tiny guillotines; they are precise yet anonymous. When I fold in chopped olives whose oilcomb graves still smell of the tree near Priene that Herodotus once claimed sang before storms, the philosophical cash register rings: splice the sea into the cosmos and you obtain lunch. The olives squirt burgundy flecks onto the flour dust: domestic galaxies arrayed on linoleum. To prevent sticking I sprinkle semolina like beach coral. One grain adheres to my thumbnail and, attached now, resembles a bifurcated cricket leg. I am momentarily the proud owner of an entire entomology. The floor cumulus of flour resembles TV static when I step back. Behind me, the levain gurgles— submarine slides in the Arctic waking iceberg mothers. Somewhere a child practices trumpet— in some apartment chlorophyll window. The brass ricochets into the hallway, becomes gold dust suspended over cafeteria tapioca that no one eats because it tastes like mortuary. Culture expanding into the straw of public space; the sound reverberates like sour note foam above dough.

Meanwhile, I remember a philosophy seminar where the professor posed this riddle: suppose you could press a button that would, right now, release across Wi-Fi a perfect rendition of Bach’s Concerto in D minor, minor third movement, to every headphone connected on earth, uncensorable, unmuteable, for three minutes. Would you? One student invoked Kant. Another calculated Mechanical License royalties owed in per-unit fractions of Kazakhstani togrog. Someone proposed crowdsourced panic: parents unable to shout across bedrooms, surgeons in mid-suture hearing inexplicable harpsichord, would-be insomniacs seduced back toward cataclysmic dreams. The professor asked: What is the ethical valence of gift if the gift arrives whether the recipient asked or not? The class tabled the question, but two weeks later the campus network exhibited an audio glitch in which bits of the Brandenburg slipped into the hold music at financial aid. No one claimed credit; the crisis dissolved into office apocrypha. Bread, unlike Bach, requires consent. You must chew.

Some evenings I walk the East River just to watch ferries slide out against the caramel LED skyline. The water smells like diluted diesel. It is not epic. It is municipal. Occasional seagulls perch on buoys as yielding as marshmallows. I have decided that narrating oneself in third person is the only civic remedy for solipsism: “He noticed the moon eclipsed by a luxury condo marketing text reflected off the river.” There; metropolis documented. He—the proxy—remembers once trying to read Ashbery on a Sunday when his phone battery died. The book opened to the poem about paddle-wheel steamers. The paper smelled like cheap muffin liners. The line “There are two kinds of people, those who wash the grapes and those who eat grapes with pesticide” felt prophetic in the way only nonsense becomes prophetic when archives bottom out. At the water’s edge he opened the random line into allegory. Washing grapes is middle-class comfort in the face of oblivion; eating them raw is nihilist surrender. Except most people he knew alternated modes hourly, like the Q train switching local to express without warning. Cross-eyed pigeons cooed in G minor around his sneakers. And four helicopters performed a Meredith Monk choreography above the Williamsburg Bridge. Then he walked home to see whether the levain had developed sufficient do-not-disturb fizz. It had. The apartment held the aroma of Swedish forest mushrooms. He had never been to Sweden. Still, the olfactory memory arrived prepaid from a catalogue somewhere north of Eurovision.

Last spring on Zoom a Syrian journalist described photographing bread queues in Maaret al-Numan before the city evaporated. The video call lagged; every tenth word vaporized. She held a cracked phone screen to camera through which a line of civilians curved around a bakery wall bullet-scarred into stucco acne. She said the yeast came from displaced farmers carrying wild strains in screw-top jars. The ovens burned olive branches because diesel trucks no longer reached rebel segments. A boy in the queue clapped to keep his hands warm; the sound recorded a heartbeat later shaped like cardiac snowflakes. Mixed metaphors, yes, but translation always stubs its toe on geography. She never shared the photographs afterward because the bakery was bombed three days later. Instead she posted a poem in Arabic whose first line translates roughly to “The sky charging admission in crumbs.” I wanted to inquire whether the same wheat once grew along the Euphrates in the Bronze Age under cuneiform inventories like “barley, woman’s ration: 20 liters.” Instead I remained muted, camera off, clutching a buttery notebook in which only bread recipes and extinct beetle Latin appear. My modem blinking amber like a medieval rood-screen. She does not know that before sleep I whisper the poem phonetically syllable by syllable against the bedroom wall, polyglot incantation as payment against debt accrued by being alive on cable internet.

Are these digressions? Maybe the point is to follow them the way the point of whale song is to follow oceanic tectonics, not to end but to calibrate circulation. I have tried cataloguing the present the way a census taker might have tabulated canal barges in 1387 Polizzi Generosa: rigorously yet arbitrarily. To count now, you would include thirty seasons of The Great British Bake Off dubbed into Catalan; photographs of prisoners baked challah in Oaxacan commissaries; Reddit threads about the impossibility of recreating Caribbean coco bread without Lilla Benbow’s evaporated milk. Arch are the ways hunger colonizes attention. Also notable: Spotify playlists titled “Dough Therapy” ensuring the same lo-fi beats repeat everywhere ATMs outgas customer anxieties. The instrumentation nudges endocrine systems toward serotonin; meanwhile Spotify employee stock vests when quarterly skin-in-the-game metrics score vertical integration goals. Somewhere the server farm siphons hydroelectric energy from waterfalls kept intact to service the illusion that flatbread meditation can still be free.

Last month the doctor said I might be gluten intolerant. I laughed until I cried, then cried until I laughed again. Levain shrugged. Celiac witch-hunts are real; entire bakeries display disclaimers like pubs banning card sharks. Yet I continued baking because not-baking felt like renouncing bilingual tongue. The body keeps inventory: I noted a rash along forearm vein like braille. Still I spooned levain into new dough each dawn, confident that the future will invent a pill allowing forgiveness for ancient gluten pacts. Or maybe my future self will only eat tomorrow’s gluten-free loaf, its ingredients sourced from cloned einkorn grains exhibited at that week’s Whitney retrospective next to a film loop of pastry clouds shot by drone over Chernobyl. Life will become a double exhibition: one channel displays your dietary restriction, another channel displays the exhibition’s gift shop selling elk jerky curation. The wall text will read: The body is a museum curated by historians it cannot employ.

And yet the miracle repeats. Last night before dawn the dough rose beyond rim limits of the banneton, spilled like the lazy neck of a sea-lion over old elevator shaft. I scored the hemisphere with a cut shaped like the Ugaritic letter B, ancestor of the one I use now when typing this sentence. Locked back in the oven, steam spliced by a tray of lava rocks, the slash inflated into a petal articulating wide as subway stairs. Listening by the door latch I remembered anaphora from sixth grade English: repetition, repetition, repetition. My mother kneeling in church murmuring Kyrie eleison. My father janitoring university corridors at 4 a.m. humming Brubeck bass riffs under fluorescent grids both of us lost inside our own iterations. The bread and the prayer and the mop and the horn formed a quartet without audience. Only the scar remembered the signature; by bake’s end it had blistered into inscription beyond orthography.

I remove the loaf wearing double-welders mitts. Cool on the rack, it chimes once more— a faint violin singing across bridge wood. When sliced open at hour four (scientists agree you must wait) the crumb resembles filigreed parquet lit by internal dawn. I spread miso-butter and inhale. Flavor dialectic: salt-fermented soy funk, butter grass from upstate New Zealand cows, and crust carrying note of cedar from leftover coffee stored too close to the rack. Architecture and agriculture melt into synaesthetic governance on my tongue. Meanwhile an email arrives: subscription to yeast data-analytics newsletter; an ad offers to optimize hydration via satellite. I laugh so hard a morsel flies across room landing atop my grandmother’s book-sewn table runner brought from Marche farmhouse where the cloth once served altar linens during wartime occupation, then wedding feast, then refugee resettlement luggage. Every crumb therefore gets diplomatic immunity. It is free to roam horizontally across license agreements.

You could argue all writing is exile and this bread is one long letter home whose address refuses to stay still. The envelopes thick as crust blister with postmarks from coordinates I keep revisiting, only to discover a new Metro station named after a jazz musician, a riverside bar with no memory of the flooding that buried the original, a corner lot where once stood a speakeasy whose cocktail napkins read Bread Is The Warmest Word unless you hold them up to mirror, whereupon the reflection says Drawn Wardrobe Myth Sere. Thus poetry is born in misprint.

Consider translation again: not between languages but between positions—of vantage, of appetite, of impatience. To the oven, the loaves are corridors the baker is not allowed to walk through; to the microbes inside dough, oven heat is apocalypse. To the baker, the living room couch is salvation. To the cat who will one day knock over banneton when I turn away, it is a playground. These are not metaphors. They are maps of attention overlaid on one another like acetate animations flickering. Zeppelins slide across mid-town traffic update. Dolphins evacuate the Hudson ghost channel. Pollen prints a semaphore of minor thirds across subway resettlement. What remains is the sound of yeast gnawing away at tomorrow’s memory, a dental chisel on slate that accelerates until you realize it is actually the susurrus of your own blood.

Isn’t that the point of returning to ancient carbohydrates? To reenact the equation Self divided by Collective equals savor plus remainder multiplied by whatever spices silence. If history teaches material resistance, bread teaches material surrender. You cede control to protein networks and temperature rhythms, and in exchange the dough grants you civilization: first portable technology, first refundable religion, first universal emoji— round sustenance low on x-axis, steam smiling above. The child with whom I once played marbles invented symbolic currency from crumbs. Each crumb equaled one hope. He is now a currency trader in Singapore. Last week he messaged through WhatsApp that he still carries a folded, stale heel inside his piano bench drawer, not for investment but for reminder. I asked whether the reminder reminds. He replied only with a voice memo one second long of himself silence—soft rush like distant dough rising. Currency is not medium of exchange. Currency is an infinite deferral of arrival.

Because cities are laminated, you keep unpeeling them at 3 a.m. I peeled the Lower East Side market last week and found one bodega selling instant ramen flavors only written in Cyrillic; I peeled again and discovered the bodega owner hosting a reading group of Anna Karenina on Wickr, each stanza redacted like classified NSA PowerPoint. A child skateboarded past cartons of guava ice pops humming the Tetris theme backwards. Somewhere deeper, under the bodega floorboard, satellites detect copper piping hydraulics once utilized by bootleg distillers during Prohibition whose gin tasted of bread heel dissolved in rainwater. The laminate gives off no complaint. The city is a palimpsestic panini—press again and out squirts another layer, warm and fragrant: beggar kings, Yiddish anarchists, cybernetic scooters, the early 2000s pop song broadcasting from a convertible parked under DIY shower of blossoms of unknown horticultural sponsorship. Blossoms fall, stick to taxi hoods, become artisanal confetti. Tourists film. Hamsters on leashes film back. Everyone co-writes the script tagged #cityasoralarchive.

What does the archive taste like? Like smoked eel chaser followed by Munich malt ending on hint of gentrified pineapple IPA. It tastes like a photon echo of neoliberal malaise powdered with nostalgia for mall pizza once consumed during senate-financed 1984 Olympics. You might call it predictable, until a twenty-something graffiti artist tags glyph onto her own palate using edible ink brewed from anthocyanins. Afterwards no two tongues read the wall identically. Each tongue remembers different brands of seltzers rescinded by market analytics teams. Each tongue abbreviates. Meanwhile drones shape asphalt topography into bioswales that mimic the lines of the Levitical temple menorah; no one notices except an urban planner on psilocybin. Every six seconds TikTok loop provides a new governance theory dipped in frosting. I bookmark, forget, remember by taste a week later when the frosting reappears atop a diner short-stack in des moines via algorithmic coupon cross-pollination. The book of Deuteronomy instructs Israelites not to harvest the corners of their fields, leaving gleanings for the poor. My phone now harvests this verse as #22 coupon code for “bread ends BOGO next Dairy Barn”.

Does the planet approve of these parentheses? The moon, being tidally locked to our own boredom, issues monthly postcards. Sometimes they are written in the language of wildfires whose smoke travels across hemispheric jet streams to laminate sunsets over northern islands where inhabitants taste California oak in the back of their throat without ever owning a passport to L.A. county. Other months the moon simply slides an invoice under the door: “Payment due for extended metaphors. Please remit two carbon credits and one low-orbit funeral.” Mimeographed copies proliferate until someone folds them origami into paper balloons launching off balcony coal-powered nostalgia tours. Somewhere those receipts land atop the landfill where archaeologists of 2587 A.D. will extract plastics compressed into geological strata resembling parlophone gramophone records. They will spin the strata using augmented reality forks and hear bootlegged voicemail in which a teenager whispers, “Mom, I miss the city that smelled like hot bread during brownouts.” Paradox: the reenactment loops at 78 rpm, but nostalgia renders the speed irrelevant. All speeds are middle speed in invisible cinema.

And so I rise before daybreak to open the oven again. Second bake, day-old dough. Nothing wastes— not recall, not loaf ends, not historicity. Inside the residual heat the interior sings differently; yesterday’s humidity has modulated during refrigerator fermentation into phrases of marmalade and funk. The crust breaks along yesterday’s incision like a page torn but readable. When I eat a corner piece, the flavor is a thesis in expanded minor key. The alley cat outside yowls because I forgot to bring yesterday’s crusts. Inside my skull the cat’s yowl transforms into chamber reverb recalling John Cage’s 4’33” performed inside an abandoned grain elevator in Saskatchewan, a performance attended only by barn swallows. Cage's score mandates listening to ambient sound as primary of absolute composition. I listen. The ambient here includes whirring literserver cooling fan, low bass rumble of a garbage truck<|reserved_token_163721|> Bread looped into New York City’s circadian hymn.

Apocrypha tells of a sixteenth-century monk who in frustration over an angel’s cryptic dream instructions finally threw his unleavened wafers into the monastery hearth. When the horrors of ensuing civil war began, villagers returned to the ashes and found the wafers had risen, puffed into misshapen memory balloons. These orphans of violence became the parents of communion loaves consumed across the valley for the next three centuries. No text survives except for the smoke drifting like dilute pencil lead across a chapel ceiling fresco depicting the burning bush. Tourists now snap filters altering the fresco sky to arctic blue, tagging #sciencemeetsfaith. In the café across the street, a barista steams milk into foam shaped like cathedral domes. He uses oat milk produced at planetary scale by genetically revolving barrels whose carbon offsets were traded for furtive asteroid mining futures. The net caloric impact remains unbalanced; no algorithm can chart the smoke dome’s imbalance, yet the monk’s humourless ash continues to raise invisible loaves somewhere between menthol and mercy.

Once you start hearing loaf as verb, everything expands. I loaf therefore I wander. I loaf therefore I wait. I loaf therefore I impeach chronological order. Consider gravity: simple desire of mass for mass. Bread is apology offered to gravity on behalf of civilisation. Yeast lifts the flour; human lifts the burden; gravity returns the crumb to mouths to ground. The cycle is not redemption but recirculation. The carbon that once coursed through dinosaurs coursing through wheat coursing through mitochondria. The smoke from the hearth carries memories of appraisal: these loaves will remind someone in 1750 Heidelberg to forgive their sister for abetting witch trial testimonies. These loaves will commemorate quarantine Yeats adaptations projected on Brooklyn rooftops during early pandemic. Between exposures exists the glow of long yellow wavelength, gentle enough to illuminate the gaps where history’s syllabus folds inside the daisy-chain codes of amino acids. History eats its own footnotes until whole epochs emerge crumb-scented.

At 2:27 a.m. insomnia reruns the footage again: an aquiline girl at track meet repeating to herself, leap, run, land. Her vocal chords vibrate at frequency doppler shifted by adolescent lungs. Later scholarship will announce she won regional long-jump but the tape stalls at abdomen over bar midair frozen like nascent sorghum risen to catch dawn. Pause. Zoom. Inside her molars swells a foam of yeast originally harvested from library dust at Alexandria when papyrus cohabited spore. Dauntless futures plot in childhood mouth unbeknownst to coaches screaming just land it. I have no footage, only mycelial goddess populating memory. Yet documented somewhere exists scintilla: the exact recombinant DNA pairing marking this young athlete as ancestral to a baker unborn who will mill grains off Europa’s future orbital husking station. Scoreboard clock blinks 2:28. I scroll Instagram instead where the girl—now woman—posts throwback photo filtered warm rustic farm grainy tweet the caption “Reminding myself what runway meant back when grass grew wild.” My thumb tap leaves digital residue of almond oil I licked from knife minutes before. Algorithm now associates nostalgia with protein bars.

Morning arrives bland and crepe. The city faces another day trading grief futures. Sun spills on FDR Drive the color of iced chai. Across river skyline looks laminated by minimalist designer. Each angle could fund museum endowments. Paragraphs like clouds pass. The oven cooling ticks metronome. Outside, anonymous passerby drops a ceramic shard on the sidewalk—pattern resembles fragment of jug from 8th century BCE Tel Megiddo. Someone else pocket-picks the shard believing it petrified sentimental value. Sirens crescendo announcing rituals of repair. Somewhere under those same sirens a delivery truck driver named Ramón delivers twelve hundred crates of Wonder Bread to shelters scheduled for demolition next month. Bread branded intangible as policy. Ramón sleeps in cab listening to flash fiction podcasts about AI nun assassins who negotiate peace via sourdough starter. His dreams redistrict into the shape of NJ turnpike cloverleaf. When he wakes, his left hand smells faintly of pool chlorine residual; in right hand cupped, a few crumbs of Wonder crust remain, soft enough to imprint his fingerprint. He eats them. He drives away. Those crumbs metabolise into the bio-matter future archaeologists sample from roadside sediment to calibrate lead contamination levels alongside farro micro-fossil evidence from 21st-century revival diets. Bread circles, circumambulates. The shard ends up re-constituted as mosaic tile five decades later adjacent to a bodega repurposed for zero-emmissions aqua-pods. None of the occupants know they walk on archaeological floor beneath their kombucha fermenting tubs stand where once stood Wonder Bread stacks. No one minds; each inhabitant receives via NFT signed jpeg of the original shard in their crypto-wallet inheritable as stardust registry.

Enough. I return to table. The knife I choose is a hundred-year-old carbon steel axe re-ground for pastry. Blade reads faint continental Braille of oxidation. Cut reveals soft crumb virtuous sponge honeycomb. Steam spirals upward momentarily visible until dispersed into general bipartisan atmospheric griefs. One end-piece toward window. One middle piece toward memory. Final piece I place as propitiation into bowl under telescope lens where the cat wanders. Cat denies ritual—sniffs, walks off, meaning I must eat the offering. I do. Extra virgin olive oil drips off fingertip onto laptop trackpad. Trackpad translates drop into click that archives poem letter O; the unmeant O remains in text forever. Thus accidental monuments proliferate. Thus the archive entombs every gesture, sterile or fertile.

Across centuries monks sing psalms shaped like dough proofing based not on rhyme scheme but gubernatorial architecture of monasteries. Their syllable-count echoed the arch ribbing. Meanwhile abbeys produced illuminated manuscripts featuring marginalia drawings of bread creatures—crawling loaves with saintly expressions, acrobat loaves flipping in air, bird-loaves regurgitating crumb communion to chick-pages. Marginalia often comedic: one margin shows a loaf writing its own cookbook in stylus held by stumpy crust paws. Under cartoon in nearly invisible ink appears line from Gilbert White’s 1789 letter: “We have lately been favored with a visit from your versatile Beaver.” Thus marginalia loops outside doctrine to attend uncanny present. I picture myself climbing margin into illumination, loafing along border while text proceeds above unaware. Such is privilege: to dwell beside narrative without owning it. Loaf as footnote, crumble as ellipsis, steam as oboe. One day metadata extractor will trace the deleted comma lying somewhere between past and past participle and discover entire medieval tapestry spun of crumbs re-notated as midi file.

I save the crust for bird feed. Nuthatches land, tweet cryptic haiku, fly. I jot: “Crust fallen / sky taken / beak translated.” What tongue remains untagged? One winter I taught English to migrants at a community library. One young woman from Homs transcribed the word bread in Syrian Arabic as “khubz.” We practiced pronunciation; the final letter curled soft like dough under her breath. In the next room ESL Johnson threaded polyester mnemonic: Keep Hands Under Bread — but her acronym failed because hands are ordained treatment for dough. Language folds memes into yeast pockets. Later we baked khubz together on cast iron under hoodless stove. Steam fogged window where neighborhood kids drew smiley faces. The loaves puffed like cartoon thought balloons. Each dent contained narrative refugee to please the mouth handing out visas admissions.

There exists history of bread and shadow of history. Conventional chronicle invokes Hammurabi, the Kyoto Bakery of 8th century, the Rhine guilds, the Paris levée du pain tax, the 1924 Wonder factory threshold of emulsifiers. Shadow history traces ants who carry crumbs home to fungal gardens under sidewalk. Shadow history follows the woman in 1972 Zabar’s line who pockets sesame seed off the press for newborns in NICU incubators. Shadow history counts epochs through the lone molecule of vanillin clinging to envelope flap from bakery milieu mailed from Prague to Detroit INS office in 1951, unfound by detector dog. Shadow history smells maple from suburban breadsticks used by suburban dads to smoke marijuana in 1978 garage while Pink Floyd’s “Wish You Were Here” skips. The chronicle publishes books; shadow evanesces into rumors everyone tasted at least once.

Bread ends like this: scrap the bowl of batter; lick finger; close cookbook set under brick to act as ledger weight. Outside, dawn trumpet inaugurates new mayor promising to fill potholes with compost containing artisanal mill sweepings. Coffee brews tide rip across enamel mug. Momentarily I belong nowhere between insomnia and bakery. Crow above rooftop pronounces bread in crow. I answer in onion powder. Thus negotiations between species proceed. Levain continues blinking slow-form Jackson Pollock on shelf. Oven cools until thermostat clicks off, a sigh. Inside memory the first communion loaf remains untouched on altar because the priest forgot to consecrate. Centuries later a child pockets it, rolls like marble down cathedral aisle into crypt description. There mold grows but mold is only rose remembered in plant tongue.

Enough for now. The word limit approaches memory’s crust edge. What I intended to say when I stepped onto the fire escape was merely goodbye to yesterday, but bread requires more. In order to close you must tear the loaf and pass the halves. I tear it North-South along the meridian across equatorial central valley where olive stellae map into micro-valleys. I eat sidewall, east-facing, then west facing to balance hemisphere. Crumbs accumulate in beard—tiny planetary rings around an aging Saturn. I offer the second half to whichever neighbor is awake; perhaps it will be Ramón returning truck from night shift, or the Syrian journalist now safely in Jersey City, or simply the hallway itself—the echoing stretch of pigeons nesting in firehose pipe. Whoever receives, inherit long narrative yeast attending every crust. Eat, I say, remember the archive. And—because language also devours us—swallow the distance between where you stand and where memory begins.

message.content:
The other night, near midnight, I stepped out onto the fire-escape in order to decide whether I still lived in the same city I thought I lived in. New York specializes in self-reinvention, but that is ordinarily a solid, daylight project—steel rising, facades reborn, zoning hearings where citizens shout. What struck me in the dark, though, was not the audible jackhammer but its opposite: the silence that replaces a giant piece of terra-cotta balcony that used to hold rain in the shape of stone acanthus leaves, now held together instead by mesh safety netting the workers forgot to remove. Under that industrial cocoon, nothing stirred. Somewhere between the nineteenth-century tenements and the twenty-first-century glass box directly across the street, an unmarked interval of time had opened up, and I found myself living inside it.

That pocket of historical silence is, in miniature, the condition of the present. No moment of the past is ever fully gone, and the future comes bristling with the invoices of the past: utility bills, algorithmic debts, thyroid counts from atmospheric testing, DNA loops it was easier to suture than to delete. Every place you stand is sedimentary paper layered so evenly you one atop another that you feel depth only at the edges, like pages cut in error and leaving a glimpse of something earlier. You flip the page and older ink bleeds through: a concentration-camp blueprint, a children’s menu from a Concord resort in 1927, shipping labels for king-crab legs, protest photos from right now shared with you by someone in another time zone. The stack is unreadable en masse; by lexis it is encyclopedic.

I have been thinking about layering because I am making bread again after a fifteen-year hiatus and the rhythm of folding dough reintroduces me to the documentary nature of repetition; every time you press the heel of your hand into the dough and pull the far edge toward you, you leave a little claw-print of autobiography. This dough will rise overnight, which means that somewhere in its genome of gluten and yeast the hours I spend asleep will be translated into taste. If I let the dough sit in the refrigerator for a week, it gains a faint vinegar sting; if I stretch it with olive oil and bake it hard, it becomes a commercial-grade cracker. The sour note becomes a chronicle of private patience. I am not in control of its bacterial parliament; my only office is temperature manager and cloudy-gazer.

Bread is only flour and water extended in time the way sound is only air extended in time, but both produce a metabolic echo inside us, pleasure that begins long before the phrase reaches the brain. The kind of bread I am making is entirely unglamorous: 75 percent hydration, 80 percent white bread flour, 20 percent red whole wheat, teaspoon of salt, dollop of levain older than some friendships. When the slab first emerges it looks like a down jacket deflating—proof that expansiveness can also be temporary. I carve my initials inside with the lame, though an hour later the seam has scarred over and will not shout anymore. The crust, if you know how to listen, sings—a high glissando as the starch retrogrades and cracks open lattice lines—so a loaf of bread is also a piece of porous choir. The apartment smells gamy enough to send an imaginary dog howling.

But memory. The smell now drags a thread back thirty-five years to a summer morning in a bakery on the Via degli Orefici in Bologna, in which I—nine years old, half Ferrara dialect, half American curiosity—watched a man pound dough as big as a suitcase against floured marble, the slap echoing off medieval vaulting. That sound combined with the cathedral intensity of light coming through the doorway, sliced gold by hanging mozzarella, first gave me the idea that labor could be a form of hieroglyphics. The man’s forearm bore tattoos rendering anchors and naked women in blue; my Ohio grandparents thought tattoos were things of secrets only soldiers kept; I did not yet know how porous private rituals become public stories. The man’s hands were bread; his hands were stories; he delivered a ciabatta to the counter steaming so turbulently that the crust blistered under a film of condensation you could map like relief topography. I never ate such a ciabatta again, but the years have brought successive approximants, and each one is a rewriting and also an apology to that original.

This is how heritage works, not blood but bakery. I belong to three places at once: the wheat bisons of North American prairies milled by unpronounceable conglomerates, the Adriatic salt left by retreating Panonian clay, and the miraculous fungi mingling immigrant sourdoughs in a jar on a Brooklyn windowsill. When I opened that jar this morning a puff of sulfur suggested that some complicated negotiation occurs nightly between Ukrainian flours and Chinatown’s octave of yeasts. The account is forever open-ended. Heritage does not ask permission; it simply balances its guttural debts across time zones. In that respect the levain behaves like the internet’s algorithmic unconscious, digesting fragments of human speech and retching them back as sponsored articles, except that the levain never forgets to stop before it blackens into ink.

Sometimes I speak to the jar. (One must animate what otherwise the ghost of pasteurization creeps.) Hello, I say, how did the October go? Has anyone written you into folklore yet? Did the apartment’s humidity attend the meeting? A levain does not represent a knowable population count but rather a momentum, a tendency. When I fail to feed it after eight days it subsides and acquires a medieval sobriety. A weaker person would mourn the injured child of civilization. I revive it anyway. The resurrection smells like bananas and alcohol and rennet: biological metaphysics. It goes back to sleep behind an elastic band and a crossed sign of prefectural origin.

If a boy can inherit a voice composed of wheat and foam and a Borgesian city at night, then surely he can inherit a book of landlessness too, a passport of questions. When I left the Midwest for good—instead of the weekend, instead of the summer—my grandfather cuff-linked my fifteen-year-old shoulder and said, The thing about getting far away is you get close to home second-hand. He meant maps. He meant that the world is wrapped around the globe like the glutinous shell of a dumpling, and once you pierce it every region leaks a broth that tastes familiar no matter where you draw the knife. His own generation had gone to war to return with photostatic memories clicked into stereoscopic viewers: St. Paul’s dome unscarred, Brandenburg Gate with no wall behind it, cherry trees in the Maritime provinces raining on submarines. In those reels I first learned the second person plural— we, the viewers against sorrow—but the syntactic fuse shivered when they clicked to the next slide: rows of nameless Korean women filing past a rail depot. The collective pronoun corkscrewed into private vertigo. A bird the color of winter flew across the ceiling. That vertigo has kept returning like yeast notes that cannot be proven accountable for anything yet reassert themselves in my dreams of train stations.

The paradox of distance is distance itself remains a place subject to tourism. Take Greenland. I have never been. On map apps it appears as an exaggerated inhale, an ice lung crushed between continents. Satellite imagery renders it baby-blue even in winter, a color that occurs nowhere inside our retinas except through prisms. In corporate pitches Greenland is simultaneously mineral heap and last Eden; in video streams young people set up camping stoves on tidewater fronts that no longer freeze. I discover that among NASA climate boards “the greening of Greenland” is a private joke wrapped in an epitaph. Yet my thumb keeps scrolling until the glacier face becomes wallpaper for a phone that is heating up to exactly the temperature recommended for proofing dough. Somewhere the screen overlays a notification: “Your yeast is ready.” My brain stitches two unrelated latitudes into one irrational whole: can I bake bread whose crumb mirrors a calving glacier? Could the steam registers be tuned to tidal chronology, so that every pocket of fermentation records the audible pop of methane bubbles bursting in thawing permafrost? The recipe would need salt mined from the tears of sixteenth-century Basque cod fishermen, who once watched their wooden ovens warp and leak in the storm off Labrador. Yet here I am again, tethered by a copper wire running inside a kitchen wall older than the idea of pixels.

I sometimes suspect that the bare linguistic phrase “I don’t know where I am going” has more mileage than any life plan. Not-knowing is the black truffle folded into the sauce: valuable precisely because it is underground, risky if overused, overrated if subjected to promiscuous fetish. When you taste it you taste the baobab of mystery in one molecule, and then it finishes into coarser prosaic bedding—that is what nostalgia does to time. In high school we had a physics teacher who spent one entire period drawing on the whiteboard a triangle the way textbooks draw an iceberg: 90 percent below the water of consciousness. “That,” he said, “is the future.” One bold student asked what the ten percent above represented. The teacher moved the marker across the surface to leave a circular hole. “Today,” he answered. The classroom smelled of green marker and jealousy, as though knowledge belonged only to vertices and everything else was irritating static. But I smuggled away a question that has not stopped growing: if the triangle were placed on graph paper, what unit marks the ordinate of missing knowledge? Only later did I realize the teacher had borrowed the metaphor from whatever English department was failing him: after all, for the Titanic the visible future lasted precisely forty seconds after first impact.

That same winter my friend Sarah typed a poem onto dot-matrix printer paper torn into strips and taped to the lockers. The strips read as single words—beach, blood, porcelain, polio—at vertical intervals, so that you encountered them one jog at a time on the way to chemistry lab. We understood her target was the escalator muscle we use to ascend from locker row toward adulthood, every step aerating another graveyard vowel. When taped back together, the strips formed a longer poem about a blind girl waking on shore to a tide of syringes washed upstream from a hospital barge. It was schlock sensationalism. The principal threatened expulsion. But held up to the fluorescent ceiling the long sheet became translucent and the cryptic spaces between lines let through an older light: someone’s grandmother’s handwriting in carbon copies of ration recipes from Leningrad 1942. The laminate reflected the future backwards into the past, and honesty slipped out only as residue. The poem’s ingredient list: scarcity plus embarrassment equals voice. We never discovered who rescued the shreds; they disappeared over weekend. Still the locker room smelled of soldered memory, a faintly erroneous brine not unlike the lava-salt I now use to cover focaccia fingerprints before baking.

Years after, in London, I found the echo in the Churchill War Rooms, where the acoustics carry the twenty-four-hour metronome of a twentieth-century switchboard operator who once plugged cables for RAF fighter commands. An elderly guard pointed out the grease stains behind a map of the Barents Sea that lasted across the war. The stains were caused by the elbow of the woman who had to sit so close she wore the paper away without noticing. That woman left no diary. Only the negative impression. What is biography that is not text; biography that is a scratch in absence. I bought a postcard on the way out: same map, minus her elbow, commercially wholesome. The dictum “Never Forget” has always meant that forgetting is the curated item. I carried a little plastic bag of edible souvenirs: a piece of stout-soaked Welsh cheddar whose rind retained burlap threads, plus a booklet of postage stamps commemorating extinct beetle species. They rattled in my pocket next to an Oyster card that believed it contained £1.60. Late on the Piccadilly line a girl unfolded a paper sculpture from behind her ear—cranes made of receipts—and handed them to strangers, each bird stamped with the words Forgive me I didn’t mean to overhear. Nobody spoke the typo. By the time she reached the end of the carriage the birds had formed a small migrating flock under the strip lighting. I understood then that souvenirs are messages you send forward to yourself from the exact point you forgot what you were supposed to understand.

Bread again because dough does not pretend to contain a moral lesson. I stretch it on parchment into a rectangle. The digital scale shows grams like tiny guillotines; they are precise yet anonymous. When I fold in chopped olives whose oilcomb graves still smell of the tree near Priene that Herodotus once claimed sang before storms, the philosophical cash register rings: splice the sea into the cosmos and you obtain lunch. The olives squirt burgundy flecks onto the flour dust: domestic galaxies arrayed on linoleum. To prevent sticking I sprinkle semolina like beach coral. One grain adheres to my thumbnail and, attached now, resembles a bifurcated cricket leg. I am momentarily the proud owner of an entire entomology. The floor cumulus of flour resembles TV static when I step back. Behind me, the levain gurgles— submarine slides in the Arctic waking iceberg mothers. Somewhere a child practices trumpet— in some apartment chlorophyll window. The brass ricochets into the hallway, becomes gold dust suspended over cafeteria tapioca that no one eats because it tastes like mortuary. Culture expanding into the straw of public space; the sound reverberates like sour note foam above dough.

Meanwhile, I remember a philosophy seminar where the professor posed this riddle: suppose you could press a button that would, right now, release across Wi-Fi a perfect rendition of Bach’s Concerto in D minor, minor third movement, to every headphone connected on earth, uncensorable, unmuteable, for three minutes. Would you? One student invoked Kant. Another calculated Mechanical License royalties owed in per-unit fractions of Kazakhstani togrog. Someone proposed crowdsourced panic: parents unable to shout across bedrooms, surgeons in mid-suture hearing inexplicable harpsichord, would-be insomniacs seduced back toward cataclysmic dreams. The professor asked: What is the ethical valence of gift if the gift arrives whether the recipient asked or not? The class tabled the question, but two weeks later the campus network exhibited an audio glitch in which bits of the Brandenburg slipped into the hold music at financial aid. No one claimed credit; the crisis dissolved into office apocrypha. Bread, unlike Bach, requires consent. You must chew.

Some evenings I walk the East River just to watch ferries slide out against the caramel LED skyline. The water smells like diluted diesel. It is not epic. It is municipal. Occasional seagulls perch on buoys as yielding as marshmallows. I have decided that narrating oneself in third person is the only civic remedy for solipsism: “He noticed the moon eclipsed by a luxury condo marketing text reflected off the river.” There; metropolis documented. He—the proxy—remembers once trying to read Ashbery on a Sunday when his phone battery died. The book opened to the poem about paddle-wheel steamers. The paper smelled like cheap muffin liners. The line “There are two kinds of people, those who wash the grapes and those who eat grapes with pesticide” felt prophetic in the way only nonsense becomes prophetic when archives bottom out. At the water’s edge he opened the random line into allegory. Washing grapes is middle-class comfort in the face of oblivion; eating them raw is nihilist surrender. Except most people he knew alternated modes hourly, like the Q train switching local to express without warning. Cross-eyed pigeons cooed in G minor around his sneakers. And four helicopters performed a Meredith Monk choreography above the Williamsburg Bridge. Then he walked home to see whether the levain had developed sufficient do-not-disturb fizz. It had. The apartment held the aroma of Swedish forest mushrooms. He had never been to Sweden. Still, the olfactory memory arrived prepaid from a catalogue somewhere north of Eurovision.

Last spring on Zoom a Syrian journalist described photographing bread queues in Maaret al-Numan before the city evaporated. The video call lagged; every tenth word vaporized. She held a cracked phone screen to camera through which a line of civilians curved around a bakery wall bullet-scarred into stucco acne. She said the yeast came from displaced farmers carrying wild strains in screw-top jars. The ovens burned olive branches because diesel trucks no longer reached rebel segments. A boy in the queue clapped to keep his hands warm; the sound recorded a heartbeat later shaped like cardiac snowflakes. Mixed metaphors, yes, but translation always stubs its toe on geography. She never shared the photographs afterward because the bakery was bombed three days later. Instead she posted a poem in Arabic whose first line translates roughly to “The sky charging admission in crumbs.” I wanted to inquire whether the same wheat once grew along the Euphrates in the Bronze Age under cuneiform inventories like “barley, woman’s ration: 20 liters.” Instead I remained muted, camera off, clutching a buttery notebook in which only bread recipes and extinct beetle Latin appear. My modem blinking amber like a medieval rood-screen. She does not know that before sleep I whisper the poem phonetically syllable by syllable against the bedroom wall, polyglot incantation as payment against debt accrued by being alive on cable internet.

Are these digressions? Maybe the point is to follow them the way the point of whale song is to follow oceanic tectonics, not to end but to calibrate circulation. I have tried cataloguing the present the way a census taker might have tabulated canal barges in 1387 Polizzi Generosa: rigorously yet arbitrarily. To count now, you would include thirty seasons of The Great British Bake Off dubbed into Catalan; photographs of prisoners baked challah in Oaxacan commissaries; Reddit threads about the impossibility of recreating Caribbean coco bread without Lilla Benbow’s evaporated milk. Arch are the ways hunger colonizes attention. Also notable: Spotify playlists titled “Dough Therapy” ensuring the same lo-fi beats repeat everywhere ATMs outgas customer anxieties. The instrumentation nudges endocrine systems toward serotonin; meanwhile Spotify employee stock vests when quarterly skin-in-the-game metrics score vertical integration goals. Somewhere the server farm siphons hydroelectric energy from waterfalls kept intact to service the illusion that flatbread meditation can still be free.

Last month the doctor said I might be gluten intolerant. I laughed until I cried, then cried until I laughed again. Levain shrugged. Celiac witch-hunts are real; entire bakeries display disclaimers like pubs banning card sharks. Yet I continued baking because not-baking felt like renouncing bilingual tongue. The body keeps inventory: I noted a rash along forearm vein like braille. Still I spooned levain into new dough each dawn, confident that the future will invent a pill allowing forgiveness for ancient gluten pacts. Or maybe my future self will only eat tomorrow’s gluten-free loaf, its ingredients sourced from cloned einkorn grains exhibited at that week’s Whitney retrospective next to a film loop of pastry clouds shot by drone over Chernobyl. Life will become a double exhibition: one channel displays your dietary restriction, another channel displays the exhibition’s gift shop selling elk jerky curation. The wall text will read: The body is a museum curated by historians it cannot employ.

And yet the miracle repeats. Last night before dawn the dough rose beyond rim limits of the banneton, spilled like the lazy neck of a sea-lion over old elevator shaft. I scored the hemisphere with a cut shaped like the Ugaritic letter B, ancestor of the one I use now when typing this sentence. Locked back in the oven, steam spliced by a tray of lava rocks, the slash inflated into a petal articulating wide as subway stairs. Listening by the door latch I remembered anaphora from sixth grade English: repetition, repetition, repetition. My mother kneeling in church murmuring Kyrie eleison. My father janitoring university corridors at 4 a.m. humming Brubeck bass riffs under fluorescent grids both of us lost inside our own iterations. The bread and the prayer and the mop and the horn formed a quartet without audience. Only the scar remembered the signature; by bake’s end it had blistered into inscription beyond orthography.

I remove the loaf wearing double-welders mitts. Cool on the rack, it chimes once more— a faint violin singing across bridge wood. When sliced open at hour four (scientists agree you must wait) the crumb resembles filigreed parquet lit by internal dawn. I spread miso-butter and inhale. Flavor dialectic: salt-fermented soy funk, butter grass from upstate New Zealand cows, and crust carrying note of cedar from leftover coffee stored too close to the rack. Architecture and agriculture melt into synaesthetic governance on my tongue. Meanwhile an email arrives: subscription to yeast data-analytics newsletter; an ad offers to optimize hydration via satellite. I laugh so hard a morsel flies across room landing atop my grandmother’s book-sewn table runner brought from Marche farmhouse where the cloth once served altar linens during wartime occupation, then wedding feast, then refugee resettlement luggage. Every crumb therefore gets diplomatic immunity. It is free to roam horizontally across license agreements.

You could argue all writing is exile and this bread is one long letter home whose address refuses to stay still. The envelopes thick as crust blister with postmarks from coordinates I keep revisiting, only to discover a new Metro station named after a jazz musician, a riverside bar with no memory of the flooding that buried the original, a corner lot where once stood a speakeasy whose cocktail napkins read Bread Is The Warmest Word unless you hold them up to mirror, whereupon the reflection says Drawn Wardrobe Myth Sere. Thus poetry is born in misprint.

Consider translation again: not between languages but between positions—of vantage, of appetite, of impatience. To the oven, the loaves are corridors the baker is not allowed to walk through; to the microbes inside dough, oven heat is apocalypse. To the baker, the living room couch is salvation. To the cat who will one day knock over banneton when I turn away, it is a playground. These are not metaphors. They are maps of attention overlaid on one another like acetate animations flickering. Zeppelins slide across mid-town traffic update. Dolphins evacuate the Hudson ghost channel. Pollen prints a semaphore of minor thirds across subway resettlement. What remains is the sound of yeast gnawing away at tomorrow’s memory, a dental chisel on slate that accelerates until you realize it is actually the susurrus of your own blood.

Isn’t that the point of returning to ancient carbohydrates? To reenact the equation Self divided by Collective equals savor plus remainder multiplied by whatever spices silence. If history teaches material resistance, bread teaches material surrender. You cede control to protein networks and temperature rhythms, and in exchange the dough grants you civilization: first portable technology, first refundable religion, first universal emoji— round sustenance low on x-axis, steam smiling above. The child with whom I once played marbles invented symbolic currency from crumbs. Each crumb equaled one hope. He is now a currency trader in Singapore. Last week he messaged through WhatsApp that he still carries a folded, stale heel inside his piano bench drawer, not for investment but for reminder. I asked whether the reminder reminds. He replied only with a voice memo one second long of himself silence—soft rush like distant dough rising. Currency is not medium of exchange. Currency is an infinite deferral of arrival.

Because cities are laminated, you keep unpeeling them at 3 a.m. I peeled the Lower East Side market last week and found one bodega selling instant ramen flavors only written in Cyrillic; I peeled again and discovered the bodega owner hosting a reading group of Anna Karenina on Wickr, each stanza redacted like classified NSA PowerPoint. A child skateboarded past cartons of guava ice pops humming the Tetris theme backwards. Somewhere deeper, under the bodega floorboard, satellites detect copper piping hydraulics once utilized by bootleg distillers during Prohibition whose gin tasted of bread heel dissolved in rainwater. The laminate gives off no complaint. The city is a palimpsestic panini—press again and out squirts another layer, warm and fragrant: beggar kings, Yiddish anarchists, cybernetic scooters, the early 2000s pop song broadcasting from a convertible parked under DIY shower of blossoms of unknown horticultural sponsorship. Blossoms fall, stick to taxi hoods, become artisanal confetti. Tourists film. Hamsters on leashes film back. Everyone co-writes the script tagged #cityasoralarchive.

What does the archive taste like? Like smoked eel chaser followed by Munich malt ending on hint of gentrified pineapple IPA. It tastes like a photon echo of neoliberal malaise powdered with nostalgia for mall pizza once consumed during senate-financed 1984 Olympics. You might call it predictable, until a twenty-something graffiti artist tags glyph onto her own palate using edible ink brewed from anthocyanins. Afterwards no two tongues read the wall identically. Each tongue remembers different brands of seltzers rescinded by market analytics teams. Each tongue abbreviates. Meanwhile drones shape asphalt topography into bioswales that mimic the lines of the Levitical temple menorah; no one notices except an urban planner on psilocybin. Every six seconds TikTok loop provides a new governance theory dipped in frosting. I bookmark, forget, remember by taste a week later when the frosting reappears atop a diner short-stack in des moines via algorithmic coupon cross-pollination. The book of Deuteronomy instructs Israelites not to harvest the corners of their fields, leaving gleanings for the poor. My phone now harvests this verse as #22 coupon code for “bread ends BOGO next Dairy Barn”.

Does the planet approve of these parentheses? The moon, being tidally locked to our own boredom, issues monthly postcards. Sometimes they are written in the language of wildfires whose smoke travels across hemispheric jet streams to laminate sunsets over northern islands where inhabitants taste California oak in the back of their throat without ever owning a passport to L.A. county. Other months the moon simply slides an invoice under the door: “Payment due for extended metaphors. Please remit two carbon credits and one low-orbit funeral.” Mimeographed copies proliferate until someone folds them origami into paper balloons launching off balcony coal-powered nostalgia tours. Somewhere those receipts land atop the landfill where archaeologists of 2587 A.D. will extract plastics compressed into geological strata resembling parlophone gramophone records. They will spin the strata using augmented reality forks and hear bootlegged voicemail in which a teenager whispers, “Mom, I miss the city that smelled like hot bread during brownouts.” Paradox: the reenactment loops at 78 rpm, but nostalgia renders the speed irrelevant. All speeds are middle speed in invisible cinema.

And so I rise before daybreak to open the oven again. Second bake, day-old dough. Nothing wastes— not recall, not loaf ends, not historicity. Inside the residual heat the interior sings differently; yesterday’s humidity has modulated during refrigerator fermentation into phrases of marmalade and funk. The crust breaks along yesterday’s incision like a page torn but readable. When I eat a corner piece, the flavor is a thesis in expanded minor key. The alley cat outside yowls because I forgot to bring yesterday’s crusts. Inside my skull the cat’s yowl transforms into chamber reverb recalling John Cage’s 4’33” performed inside an abandoned grain elevator in Saskatchewan, a performance attended only by barn swallows. Cage's score mandates listening to ambient sound as primary of absolute composition. I listen. The ambient here includes whirring literserver cooling fan, low bass rumble of a garbage truck<|reserved_token_163721|> Bread looped into New York City’s circadian hymn.

Apocrypha tells of a sixteenth-century monk who in frustration over an angel’s cryptic dream instructions finally threw his unleavened wafers into the monastery hearth. When the horrors of ensuing civil war began, villagers returned to the ashes and found the wafers had risen, puffed into misshapen memory balloons. These orphans of violence became the parents of communion loaves consumed across the valley for the next three centuries. No text survives except for the smoke drifting like dilute pencil lead across a chapel ceiling fresco depicting the burning bush. Tourists now snap filters altering the fresco sky to arctic blue, tagging #sciencemeetsfaith. In the café across the street, a barista steams milk into foam shaped like cathedral domes. He uses oat milk produced at planetary scale by genetically revolving barrels whose carbon offsets were traded for furtive asteroid mining futures. The net caloric impact remains unbalanced; no algorithm can chart the smoke dome’s imbalance, yet the monk’s humourless ash continues to raise invisible loaves somewhere between menthol and mercy.

Once you start hearing loaf as verb, everything expands. I loaf therefore I wander. I loaf therefore I wait. I loaf therefore I impeach chronological order. Consider gravity: simple desire of mass for mass. Bread is apology offered to gravity on behalf of civilisation. Yeast lifts the flour; human lifts the burden; gravity returns the crumb to mouths to ground. The cycle is not redemption but recirculation. The carbon that once coursed through dinosaurs coursing through wheat coursing through mitochondria. The smoke from the hearth carries memories of appraisal: these loaves will remind someone in 1750 Heidelberg to forgive their sister for abetting witch trial testimonies. These loaves will commemorate quarantine Yeats adaptations projected on Brooklyn rooftops during early pandemic. Between exposures exists the glow of long yellow wavelength, gentle enough to illuminate the gaps where history’s syllabus folds inside the daisy-chain codes of amino acids. History eats its own footnotes until whole epochs emerge crumb-scented.

At 2:27 a.m. insomnia reruns the footage again: an aquiline girl at track meet repeating to herself, leap, run, land. Her vocal chords vibrate at frequency doppler shifted by adolescent lungs. Later scholarship will announce she won regional long-jump but the tape stalls at abdomen over bar midair frozen like nascent sorghum risen to catch dawn. Pause. Zoom. Inside her molars swells a foam of yeast originally harvested from library dust at Alexandria when papyrus cohabited spore. Dauntless futures plot in childhood mouth unbeknownst to coaches screaming just land it. I have no footage, only mycelial goddess populating memory. Yet documented somewhere exists scintilla: the exact recombinant DNA pairing marking this young athlete as ancestral to a baker unborn who will mill grains off Europa’s future orbital husking station. Scoreboard clock blinks 2:28. I scroll Instagram instead where the girl—now woman—posts throwback photo filtered warm rustic farm grainy tweet the caption “Reminding myself what runway meant back when grass grew wild.” My thumb tap leaves digital residue of almond oil I licked from knife minutes before. Algorithm now associates nostalgia with protein bars.

Morning arrives bland and crepe. The city faces another day trading grief futures. Sun spills on FDR Drive the color of iced chai. Across river skyline looks laminated by minimalist designer. Each angle could fund museum endowments. Paragraphs like clouds pass. The oven cooling ticks metronome. Outside, anonymous passerby drops a ceramic shard on the sidewalk—pattern resembles fragment of jug from 8th century BCE Tel Megiddo. Someone else pocket-picks the shard believing it petrified sentimental value. Sirens crescendo announcing rituals of repair. Somewhere under those same sirens a delivery truck driver named Ramón delivers twelve hundred crates of Wonder Bread to shelters scheduled for demolition next month. Bread branded intangible as policy. Ramón sleeps in cab listening to flash fiction podcasts about AI nun assassins who negotiate peace via sourdough starter. His dreams redistrict into the shape of NJ turnpike cloverleaf. When he wakes, his left hand smells faintly of pool chlorine residual; in right hand cupped, a few crumbs of Wonder crust remain, soft enough to imprint his fingerprint. He eats them. He drives away. Those crumbs metabolise into the bio-matter future archaeologists sample from roadside sediment to calibrate lead contamination levels alongside farro micro-fossil evidence from 21st-century revival diets. Bread circles, circumambulates. The shard ends up re-constituted as mosaic tile five decades later adjacent to a bodega repurposed for zero-emmissions aqua-pods. None of the occupants know they walk on archaeological floor beneath their kombucha fermenting tubs stand where once stood Wonder Bread stacks. No one minds; each inhabitant receives via NFT signed jpeg of the original shard in their crypto-wallet inheritable as stardust registry.

Enough. I return to table. The knife I choose is a hundred-year-old carbon steel axe re-ground for pastry. Blade reads faint continental Braille of oxidation. Cut reveals soft crumb virtuous sponge honeycomb. Steam spirals upward momentarily visible until dispersed into general bipartisan atmospheric griefs. One end-piece toward window. One middle piece toward memory. Final piece I place as propitiation into bowl under telescope lens where the cat wanders. Cat denies ritual—sniffs, walks off, meaning I must eat the offering. I do. Extra virgin olive oil drips off fingertip onto laptop trackpad. Trackpad translates drop into click that archives poem letter O; the unmeant O remains in text forever. Thus accidental monuments proliferate. Thus the archive entombs every gesture, sterile or fertile.

Across centuries monks sing psalms shaped like dough proofing based not on rhyme scheme but gubernatorial architecture of monasteries. Their syllable-count echoed the arch ribbing. Meanwhile abbeys produced illuminated manuscripts featuring marginalia drawings of bread creatures—crawling loaves with saintly expressions, acrobat loaves flipping in air, bird-loaves regurgitating crumb communion to chick-pages. Marginalia often comedic: one margin shows a loaf writing its own cookbook in stylus held by stumpy crust paws. Under cartoon in nearly invisible ink appears line from Gilbert White’s 1789 letter: “We have lately been favored with a visit from your versatile Beaver.” Thus marginalia loops outside doctrine to attend uncanny present. I picture myself climbing margin into illumination, loafing along border while text proceeds above unaware. Such is privilege: to dwell beside narrative without owning it. Loaf as footnote, crumble as ellipsis, steam as oboe. One day metadata extractor will trace the deleted comma lying somewhere between past and past participle and discover entire medieval tapestry spun of crumbs re-notated as midi file.

I save the crust for bird feed. Nuthatches land, tweet cryptic haiku, fly. I jot: “Crust fallen / sky taken / beak translated.” What tongue remains untagged? One winter I taught English to migrants at a community library. One young woman from Homs transcribed the word bread in Syrian Arabic as “khubz.” We practiced pronunciation; the final letter curled soft like dough under her breath. In the next room ESL Johnson threaded polyester mnemonic: Keep Hands Under Bread — but her acronym failed because hands are ordained treatment for dough. Language folds memes into yeast pockets. Later we baked khubz together on cast iron under hoodless stove. Steam fogged window where neighborhood kids drew smiley faces. The loaves puffed like cartoon thought balloons. Each dent contained narrative refugee to please the mouth handing out visas admissions.

There exists history of bread and shadow of history. Conventional chronicle invokes Hammurabi, the Kyoto Bakery of 8th century, the Rhine guilds, the Paris levée du pain tax, the 1924 Wonder factory threshold of emulsifiers. Shadow history traces ants who carry crumbs home to fungal gardens under sidewalk. Shadow history follows the woman in 1972 Zabar’s line who pockets sesame seed off the press for newborns in NICU incubators. Shadow history counts epochs through the lone molecule of vanillin clinging to envelope flap from bakery milieu mailed from Prague to Detroit INS office in 1951, unfound by detector dog. Shadow history smells maple from suburban breadsticks used by suburban dads to smoke marijuana in 1978 garage while Pink Floyd’s “Wish You Were Here” skips. The chronicle publishes books; shadow evanesces into rumors everyone tasted at least once.

Bread ends like this: scrap the bowl of batter; lick finger; close cookbook set under brick to act as ledger weight. Outside, dawn trumpet inaugurates new mayor promising to fill potholes with compost containing artisanal mill sweepings. Coffee brews tide rip across enamel mug. Momentarily I belong nowhere between insomnia and bakery. Crow above rooftop pronounces bread in crow. I answer in onion powder. Thus negotiations between species proceed. Levain continues blinking slow-form Jackson Pollock on shelf. Oven cools until thermostat clicks off, a sigh. Inside memory the first communion loaf remains untouched on altar because the priest forgot to consecrate. Centuries later a child pockets it, rolls like marble down cathedral aisle into crypt description. There mold grows but mold is only rose remembered in plant tongue.

Enough for now. The word limit approaches memory’s crust edge. What I intended to say when I stepped onto the fire escape was merely goodbye to yesterday, but bread requires more. In order to close you must tear the loaf and pass the halves. I tear it North-South along the meridian across equatorial central valley where olive stellae map into micro-valleys. I eat sidewall, east-facing, then west facing to balance hemisphere. Crumbs accumulate in beard—tiny planetary rings around an aging Saturn. I offer the second half to whichever neighbor is awake; perhaps it will be Ramón returning truck from night shift, or the Syrian journalist now safely in Jersey City, or simply the hallway itself—the echoing stretch of pigeons nesting in firehose pipe. Whoever receives, inherit long narrative yeast attending every crust. Eat, I say, remember the archive. And—because language also devours us—swallow the distance between where you stand and where memory begins.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
